title: ROUGE for Summarization
concept: ROUGE measures recall of n-grams between model summary and human reference, checking if the model captured all key facts.

exercises.prompt: What is the ROUGE metric primarily evaluated using the ROUGE metric?
exercises.explain: ROUGE is the standard metric used to evaluate automatic summarization by comparing generated summaries to human-written references.

cards.title: Did the AI Miss Anything?
cards.body: When an AI summarizes a 10-page report into one paragraph, how do we know it didn't leave out the most important facts? We use a special score called ROUGE to check if the key points made it across!

cards.title: What is ROUGE?
cards.body: ROUGE is an evaluation metric that measures recall. It counts how many words or phrases (n-grams) from the human reference summary are present in the AI-generated summary, ensuring key information is not missed.

cards.title: The Shopping List Test
cards.body: Imagine you have a shopping list with 5 items. After shopping, you check how many of those 5 items you actually bought. ROUGE does the same thing: it checks how many of the key facts from the original human summary made it into the AI's summary.

cards.title: ROUGE in Action
cards.body:
Human reference: "The cat sat on the mat."
AI summary: "The cat sat."
ROUGE matches "The", "cat", and "sat". Since the AI captured 3 out of the 5 words from the reference, it got a high recall score for those words!

cards.title: High Score ≠ Good Grammar
cards.body: People often think a high ROUGE score means a summary is beautifully written. In reality, ROUGE only checks if the same words overlap. An AI summary could have scrambled grammar but still get a high ROUGE score if the key words match!

cards.title: Visualizing Recall
cards.body: Let's look at how ROUGE counts the overlapping words between a human reference and an AI summary.

right.text: "The dog chased" (3 out of 5 words captured)

exercises.prompt: What does ROUGE look for when comparing an AI summary to a human reference?

options.text: How fast the AI generates the summary
The number of exclamation marks used
The spelling accuracy of the AI

exercises.explain: ROUGE looks at n-grams, which are sequences of words (like single words or two-word pairs), to see how much they overlap between the human reference and the AI summary.

exercises.prompt: To calculate a ROUGE score, you must have a human-written reference summary to compare against.
exercises.explain: ROUGE checks for word overlap, so it requires a human-written "gold standard" reference summary to compare the AI's summary against.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe what ROUGE primarily measures.
exercises.sentence: ROUGE measures the recall of words between the AI summary and the human reference.
exercises.explain: ROUGE focuses on recall, measuring how much of the original human reference information was successfully captured by the model.

exercises.prompt: Which of these shows the correct way to use ROUGE?

options.text: Comparing the AI summary directly to the original 10-page text
Asking users to rate the summary on a scale of 1 to 5
Comparing the AI summary to a golden reference summary written by a human

exercises.explain: Example C is correct because ROUGE evaluates summaries by comparing them directly to one or more human-written reference summaries. It does not look at the original long text or user ratings.

exercises.prompt: Your AI model just summarized a long article. Why would you choose ROUGE as your main metric to evaluate its success?

options.text: It measures how creative and unique the AI's writing is
It checks if the summary has perfect grammar
It measures how many key facts from the reference summary were captured
It counts the total number of words in the AI summary

exercises.explain: ROUGE is focused on recall. It measures how many of the gold-standard facts from the human reference were successfully captured in the generated summary. This makes sure important details aren't left out!
